elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that the Kloosterman sum $\text{Kl (1,q)$ changes sign infinitely many times, as $q\rightarrow +\infty$ with at most two prime factors.}
elements.sent-2411-002: As a consequence, our result is unconditional compared with Drappeau and Maynard's (Proc.. Amer.
elements.sent-2411-003: Soc., 2019), in which the existence of Laudau-Siegel zeros is required.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our arguments contain the Selberg sieve method, spectral theory and distribution of Kloosterman sums along with previous works by Fouvry, Matom\"{a ki, Michel, Sivak-Fischler, and Xi.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Keywords Kloosterman sum; Sign change; Selberg sieve method; Spectral theory; Equidistribution MSC(2020) 11L05, 11N36(11N75, 11L20, 26D15).
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $q$ be a fixed positive integer.
elements.sent-2411-007: For arbitrary integers $m$ and $n$, the classical Kloosterman sums are defined by \begin{equation}S(m,n;q)=\sum_{a(q)\atop (a,q)=1}e\left(\frac{ma+n\overline{a}}{q}\right),\end{equation} where $e(x)=e(2\pi ix/q)$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Kloosterman sums have originated from Poincar\'{e \cite{Poincare1911 and Kloosterman \cite{Kloosterman1927 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Scholars are concerned about whether there exists some specific explicit expression or asymptotic formula? Unfortunately, these results are beyond our capabilities for now.
elements.sent-2411-010: Kloosterman sums are one of the central research interests in analytic number theory and related to numerous applications in Diophantine equations and automorphic forms.
elements.sent-2411-011: A well-known estimate from Weil \cite{Weil1948 for the individual Kloosterman sum is \begin{equation}\left|S(m,n;p)\right|\leq 2p^{1/2}(m,n,p)^{1/2},\end{equation} in general \begin{equation}\left|S(m,n;q)\right|\leq q^{1/2}(m,n,q)^{1/2}\tau(q),\end{equation} and the factor $\tau(q)$ above can be modified to $2^{\omega(q)}$($\omega(q)$ represents the number of different prime factors of $q$) by Estermann \cite{Estermann1961 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The equidistributions of Kloosterman sums are proposed by Katz \cite{Katz1980 and analogized by the Sato-Tate conjecture of elliptic curves, which we state as.}
elements.sent-2411-013: For each $f\in\mathcal{C ([0,\pi])$ and non-zero integer $a$, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{x\rightarrow +\infty}\frac{1}{\pi(x)}\sum_{p\leq x}f\left(\theta_{p}(a)\right)=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}f(\theta)\sin^2{\theta}d\theta,\end{equation} where $\theta_{p (a)\in [0,\pi]$ is so-called Kloosterman sum angle defined as \begin{equation}p^{1/2}Kl(a;p):=S(a,1;p)=2p^{1/2}\cos{\theta_{p}(a)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Later, we usually refer to the above conjecture as the ``horizontal" Sato-Tate conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-015: Katz inferred that the angles $\theta_{p (a)$(for each $a$) are equidistributed, as $x\rightarrow +\infty$, with respect to the Sato-Tate measure \begin{equation}\mu_{ST}=\frac{2}{\pi}\sin^2{\theta}d\theta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In \cite{Katz1988 , Katz showed that the numbers \begin{equation}\left\{\theta_{p}(a):1\leq a<p\right\}\end{equation} equidistribute with respect to the same measure, as $p\rightarrow +\infty$, and we call it the ``vertical" Sato-Tate law.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, a direct corollary of the conjecture is that Kloosterman sums change signs infinitely many times as $p\rightarrow +\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we mainly concern about the sign changes Kloosterman sums.
elements.sent-2411-019: Fouvry and Michel \cite{Fouvry2003}\cite{Fouvry2007 pioneering proved that \begin{equation}\left|\left\{X<q\leq 2X: Kl(1;q)\gtrless 0, \omega(q)\leq23, q \ square-free\right\}\right|\gg\frac{X}{\log X}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Subsequently, this result was improved in a whole series of papers by Sivak-Fischler \cite{Sivak-Fischler2007, Sivak-Fischler2009 , Matom\"{a ki \cite{Matomaki2011 , Xi \cite{Xi2015, Xi2018, Xi2022 by replacing 23 with 18, 15, 10 and 7.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Recently, Drappeau and Maynard \cite{Drappeau2019 proved that under the condition of the existence of Landau-Siegel zero, 23 can be reduced to 2.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Motivated by their previous works, we showed that 23 can be improved to 2, unconditionally.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our new approach is somewhat similar to Maynard's idea \cite{Maynard2015 of applying a variant of the Selberg sieve to improve Zhang's breakthrough \cite{Zhang2014 in the Twin Prime Conjecture without changing the level of the Bombieri-Vinogradov Theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our result is stated as the following.
elements.sent-2411-025: Let $q$ be any square-free number with $\omega(q)\leq2$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Then $\text{Kl (1;q)$ changes sign infinitely many times, as $q\rightarrow +\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Define Selberg sieve weight $\left(\lambda_{d}\right)$ and state as \begin{equation} \lambda_{1}=1, \left|\lambda_{d}\right|\leq 1, \lambda_{d}=0, \ if\ d>\sqrt{D}, \end{equation} where $D$ is the level of Selberg sieve.
elements.sent-2411-028: More specifically, we set $D=X^{1/2-\epsilon}$, and \begin{equation}\lambda_{d}=\mu(d)F\left(\frac{\log\left(\sqrt{D}/d\right)}{\log\left(\sqrt{D}\right)}\right),\end{equation} where $F$ is a fixed smooth function supported on $[0,1]$ and vanishes at 0 to a suitable order (related to $\kappa$ and $l$ below).
elements.sent-2411-029: Our purpose is to show \begin{equation}\left|\left\{X<q\leq 2X: Kl(1;q)\gtrless 0, \omega(q)\leq2, q \ square-free\right\}\right|\ge c_{0}\frac{X}{\log X},\end{equation} where $c_{0 >0$ is a constant.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It suffices to prove \begin{equation}R^{\pm}(X):=\sum_{n}g\left(\frac{n}{X}\right)\mu^{2}(n)\frac{\left|S(1,1;n)\right|\pm S(1,1;n)}{\sqrt{n}}\left\{\rho-\left(\frac{\kappa}{2}\right)^{\omega(n)}\right\}\left(\sum_{d\mid n\Pi_{l}}\lambda_{d}\right)^2>0,\notag\end{equation} where $g(X)$ is a fixed smooth function supported on [1,2], $\Pi_{l}$ is the product of the first $l$ primes.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we specifically take \begin{equation}\label{select number} \kappa=4,\ l=10,\ F(x)=x^{\kappa+l}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: Comparing with \cite{Xi2015 and \cite{Xi2018 , we replaced $d\mid n$ with $d\mid n\Pi_{l}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This idea is essentially due to Selberg \cite{Selberg1989 , and our purpose of doing this is to more freely select the coefficients of $R_{1 (X)$ and $R_{3 (X)$ in the following argument.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In reviewing Selberg's method within the context of the twin prime conjecture, as outlined in \cite{Selberg1989 , we encounter a sieve weight given by \begin{equation}\left(\sum_{d\mid n(n+2)}\lambda_{d}\right)^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, in the specific problem we are addressing, the prime factorization of $n+2$ remains indeterminate.
elements.sent-2411-036: Consequently, we aim to substitute $n+2$ with a finite product of primes.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, examining the proof presented in Section 5 of \cite{Xi2018 , we observe that in the sieve weight, the oscillation amplitude of $\lambda_{d}$ becomes significant when $d$ has small prime factors.}
elements.sent-2411-038: To handle this technical issue, we introduce $\Pi_{l}$ to amplify the magnitude of possible cancellation coming from $\lambda_{d}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Alternatively, if we persist with the original sieve weight summed over divisors $d$, potential improvements to our results might be achieved by improving the level of the Selberg sieve, particularly in the context of Lemma \ref{lem: trace formula for |KL| .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Nevertheless, enhancing this level presents a formidable challenge for us at present.
elements.sent-2411-041: Fortunately, by adopting another new sieve weight, we can circumvent this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-042: Now we hope $R^{\pm (X)$ has a lower bound larger than 0, as shown below \begin{equation}R^{\pm}(X)\ge \rho R_{1}(X)\pm \rho R_{2}(X)-2R_{3}(X)>0,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}R_{1}(X):=\sum_{n}g\left(\frac{n}{X}\right)\mu^{2}(n)\left|Kl(1;n)\right|\left(\sum_{d\mid n\Pi_{l}}\lambda_{d}\right)^2,\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_{2}(X):=\sum_{n}g\left(\frac{n}{X}\right)\mu^{2}(n)Kl(1;n)\left(\sum_{d\mid n\Pi_{l}}\lambda_{d}\right)^2,\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_{3}(X):=\sum_{n}g\left(\frac{n}{X}\right)\mu^{2}(n)\left|Kl(1;n)\right|\left(\frac{\kappa}{2}\right)^{\omega(n)}\left(\sum_{d\mid n\Pi_{l}}\lambda_{d}\right)^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We will discuss $R_{1 (X)$, $R_{2 (X)$ and $R_{3 (X)$ in Section \ref{lower bound for R1 , \ref{upper bound for R2 and Section \ref{upper bound for R3 , respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The relevant conclusion of $R_{1 (X)$ can be referred to \cite{Matomaki2011 and \cite{Xi2015 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For $R_{3 (X)$, it can be handled in a similar way as in \cite{Xi2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: To prove the Theorem \ref{thm: main theorem , we need the following conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-047: For any sufficiently large number $X$, there exists some constant $C_{1 >0$ such that \begin{equation}R_{1}(X)\ge (1+o(1))C_{1}\frac{X}{\left(\log X\right)^{21}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Specifically, we choose \begin{equation}C_{1}=\left(4^{10}10!\binom{14}{10}\prod_{p\mid\Pi_{10}}\log p\right)^2\times 0.0142\tilde{g}(1),\end{equation} where $g(X)$ is a fixed smooth function defined above.
elements.sent-2411-049: For any sufficiently large number $X$, we have \begin{equation}R_{2}(X)=o\left(\frac{X}{\left(\log X\right)^{21}}\right).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-059: Before presenting the following lemmas, let's first establish some definitions.

elements.sent-2411-061: For $N>1$ and $s\in\mathbb{C}\backslash \left\{0\right\}$, we further define \begin{equation}\check{F}_{N}(s):=\sum_{n\ge 0}\frac{a_{n}}{(s\log N)^n}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-071: Therefore, we have \begin{equation}\label{double residue equ 1} R=\sum_{k_{1}\ge v_{1}}\sum_{k_{2}\ge v_{2}}\frac{a_{k_{1}}b_{k_{2}}}{(\log M)^{k_{1}+k_{2}}}\mathop{Res}_{{(s_{1},s_{2})=(0,0)}}Z(s_{1},s_{2})\frac{M^{s_{1}+s_{2}}}{s_{1}^{k_{1}-v_{1}+1}s_{2}^{k_{2}-v_{2}+1}(s_{1}+s_{2})^v},\end{equation} and the residue therein is equal to \begin{equation}\frac{1}{(2\pi i)^2}\int_{C_{2}}\int_{C_{1}}\frac{Z(s_{1},s_{2})M^{s_{1}+s_{2}}}{s_{1}^{k_{1}-v_{1}+1}s_{2}^{k_{2}-v_{2}+1}(s_{1}+s_{2})^v}ds_{1}ds_{2},\end{equation} where $C_{1}$, $C_{2}$ are the circles $|s_{1 |=\rho$ and $|s_{2 |=2\rho$ with a small $\rho>0$.}}

elements.sent-2411-118: Therefore, after shifting the $w-$contour and $s-$contour through the same way, we only need to handle the integrand with $t_{1}$, $t_{2}$ as \begin{equation}\tilde{g}(1) G(1)\sum_{j=1}^{\kappa}\binom{\kappa}{j}\sum_{i=0}^{j-1}\binom{j-1}{i}(\log X)^{j-i-1}(-1)^i\frac{\Gamma(i+j)}{\Gamma^2(j)}\notag \times\check{F}_{\sqrt{D}}(t_{1})\check{F}_{\sqrt{D}}(t_{2})K(0,t_{1},t_{2},0)I(0,t_{1},t_{2},0)(t_{1}t_{2})^{j-1}\frac{\sqrt{D}^{t_{1}+t_{2}}}{(t_{1}+t_{2})^{i+j}}.\notag\end{equation} Note that \begin{equation}K(0,0,0,0)=\frac{1}{G(1)},\end{equation} and $I(0,t_{1 ,t_{2 ,0)$ satisfies the condition in Lemma \ref{lem: double residue .}}}
